content: IP allowlisting pitfalls: (1) CRITICAL: X-Forwarded-For is trivially spoofable — any attacker can add a fake XFF header; only trust IPs appended by known trusted proxies (Vercel/Cloudflare); (2) Multiple XFF headers must be treated as a single list; using only the last header means trusting attacker-controlled values; (3) IPv4-mapped IPv6 addresses (::ffff:10.0.0.1) bypass string-match IP checks unless normalized to IPv4 form; (4) Neon serverless and Vercel add their own proxy hops — test what the actual source IP looks like before writing matching logic; (5) IPv6 allows attacker to enumerate vast address space bypassing per-IP rate limits; (6) CIDR ranges go stale — Cloudflare and Vercel update their IP ranges; hardcoded lists cause either allow-all or deny-all failures; (7) Allowlist enforcement is application-layer, not network-layer — cannot be relied upon alone for high-stakes access; combine with short-lived session tokens.
